coredata.dc:description: Nanozyme belonging to artificial enzyme is a term describing nanomaterial with enzyme-like characteristics. Great research advances have been acquired in the field of nanozymes due to their striking merits. Inspired by natural enzymes that disrupt the structural integrity of cells or interfere with metabolism, nanozymes which can effectively avoid generation of bacterial resistance may become potential alternatives for antibiotics in the context of the continuous emergence and rapid spread of drug-resistant bacteria and the slow development of new antibiotics. Naturally, nanozymes inevitably have some inherent defects, which need to be compensated by forming composite materials with other components to play a synergistic effect. What’s more, nanozyme-based composite materials retain the advantages of nanozymes and integrate multiple functions into a single system to achieve an intelligent and multi-functional therapeutic model. This is a new strategy for combating bacteria/biofilms in the future. In this review, firstly we cover the general mechanisms and design principles of nanozyme-based composite materials for fighting against bacteria/biofilms and the typical types of nanozymes for resisting bacteria/biofilms. Meanwhile the applications and the advantages of nanozyme-based composite materials for anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms are emphasized. Finally, the challenges and prospects of nanozyme-based composite materials for combating bacteria/biofilms are discussed for future research in this field.

head.ce:title: Research progress in nanozyme-based composite materials for fighting against bacteria and biofilms

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Polymer Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Polymer Science and Engineering
State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Typical types of nanozymes for fighting against bacteria and biofilms.

Nanozyme belonging to artificial enzyme is a term describing nanomaterial with enzyme-like characteristics. Great research advances have been acquired in the field of nanozymes due to their striking merits. Inspired by natural enzymes that disrupt the structural integrity of cells or interfere with metabolism, nanozymes which can effectively avoid generation of bacterial resistance may become potential alternatives for antibiotics in the context of the continuous emergence and rapid spread of drug-resistant bacteria and the slow development of new antibiotics. Naturally, nanozymes inevitably have some inherent defects, which need to be compensated by forming composite materials with other components to play a synergistic effect. What’s more, nanozyme-based composite materials retain the advantages of nanozymes and integrate multiple functions into a single system to achieve an intelligent and multi-functional therapeutic model. This is a new strategy for combating bacteria/biofilms in the future. In this review, firstly we cover the general mechanisms and design principles of nanozyme-based composite materials for fighting against bacteria/biofilms and the typical types of nanozymes for resisting bacteria/biofilms. Meanwhile the applications and the advantages of nanozyme-based composite materials for anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms are emphasized. Finally, the challenges and prospects of nanozyme-based composite materials for combating bacteria/biofilms are discussed for future research in this field.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria invade in all aspects of human life [1]. Inhalations of infectious air [2], wound infections [3], contact with contaminated medical equipment [4], accidental consumption of spoiled food [5], etc. could cause bacterial infections. Bacteria intrude into the blood circulation system to grow and reproduce, which can secrete toxins and other metabolites causing acute systemic infection, will lead to a variety of diseases and even lead to death [6]. All organs and systems of the body may be infected by bacteria [7]. It can be clinically manifested as inflammation [8], symptoms of toxemia [9], septic shock [10], and others.

Antibiotics are widely deemed to be the terminators of bacterial infections [11]. The rampant trend of the bacteria had been effectively controlled until the discovery of antibiotics [12,13]. However, the abuse and misuse of antibiotics have led to the continuous emergence and rapid spread of drug-resistant bacteria [14,15]. Currently, approximately 700,000 people die of drug-resistant bacteria infections in each year. It is predicted that by 2050, the annual death toll will rise to 10 million and the cost will reach 100 trillion dollars [16,17]. What's worse, bacteria are able to shield themselves by self-secreted extracellular polymers including polysaccharides, lipids, proteins, and extracellular DNA to form biofilm. The tolerance of biofilm to bactericide and host's immune system is approximately 10–1000 times to that of corresponding free bacteria [18].

In this case, using antibacterial materials to kill harmful bacteria and inhibit their growth and reproduction has become an important means that people widely expect for improving human health [19]. Especially, inspired by natural enzymes which catalyze to production of harmful reactive oxygen species (ROS) to cause irreversible damage to bacteria, researchers shifted their attention to building artificial enzymes with natural enzyme functions to combat bacteria [20,21]. Nanozymes, a class of nanomaterials, exhibit unexpected enzyme-like characteristics belonging to artificial enzyme. And due to the advantages of low cost, stable and mass produced compared with natural enzymes, the field of nanozymes has achieved remarkable progress [22]. However, almost all nanozymes cannot effectively interact with bacteria, and ROS has disadvantages of intrinsic short lifetime (less than 200 ns) and short diffusion distance (about 20 nm) [23,24], which will reduce the sterilization effect.

Fortunately, the above defects can be overcome through the combination of nanozymes with other components. Compared with the single component that makes up the composite materials, the overall performance of the composite materials is also improved and enhanced owing to the interactions between the various components [25]. Nanozyme-based composite materials for fighting against bacteria and biofilms not only retain the advantages of nanozymes and effectively make up for the shortcomings, but also can integrate multiple functions into a single system to achieve an intelligent and multifunctional antibacterial treatment model. In this review, we cover the design principles of nanozyme-based composite materials for anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms, typical types of nanozymes for fighting against bacteria and biofilms, and the application of nanozyme-based composite materials in anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms. Meanwhile the advantages and the challenges of nanozyme-based composite materials in anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms are emphasized. Finally, prospects of nanozyme-based composite materials combating bacteria and biofilm are also discussed for future investigation in this field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General mechanisms and design principles of nanozyme-based composite materials for combating bacteria and biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: There are many examples of defense against pathogens via enzymes induction. These enzymes are able to catalyze the reaction to produce free radicals (·OH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) or oxidation-state components which can cause irreversible damage to bacteria or the integrity of biofilms achieving anti-bacteria and anti-biofilm effects [26]. For example, xanthine oxidase in breast milk can produce superoxide anions and H2O2 in the presence of oxygen, which can gently kill bacteria, thereby protecting the intestine of newborns [27]. Lactoperoxidase in fresh milk can catalyze the oxidation of thiocyanate (SCN−) by hydrogen peroxide to form hypothionite ions (OSCN−) showing inherent natural antibacterial activity [28]. Myeloperoxidase has a higher concentration in polymorphonuclear leukocyte lysosome particles. After completion of phagocytosis, the lysosomal particle membrane in the cytoplasm and the vacuolar membrane of the phagocytic cell will fuse. After that, the membrane of the connecting part will be broken, and the components in the granule including myeloperoxidase will be released into the vacuole. Myeloperoxidase in the vacuole will catalyze the reaction of hydrogen peroxide and halides to produce singlet oxygen and hypochlorous acid, which fights against a series of bacteria and other microorganisms [29].
Nanozymes inspired by natural aim at mimic the basic and general principles of natural enzymes. Thereby, nanozymes have the potential to defend against pathogens like the natural enzymes above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General mechanisms of nanozyme-based composite materials for combating bacteria and biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: Through their own redox enzyme activity, nanozymes generate high-toxic ROS including free radicals, hydrogen peroxide, superoxide anion, singlet oxygen, or hypohalous acid in the process of catalyzing non-toxic or low-toxic substrate reactions, which can destroy the integrity of cell membranes or damage DNA in bacteria to kill a variety of bacteria (Fig. 1 ). For biofilms, nanozymes catalyze the degradation of a diversity of molecules including polysaccharides, lipids, proteins, and extracellular DNA in the biofilm matrix to eliminate the biofilm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design principles of nanozyme-based composite materials for combating bacteria and biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: Natural enzymes which can catalyze the reaction to produce ROS include peroxidase, oxidase, haloperoxide and so on [30,31]. Therefore, the design principles of nanozyme-based composite materials for combating bacteria and biofilms are mainly based on peroxidase, oxidase and haloperperoxide, etc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Based on the peroxidase-like activity of nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: Peroxidase consists of a series of enzymes, which catalyze the oxidation of substrates using peroxides (mainly hydrogen peroxide) as electron acceptors (Scheme 1 ) [32]. Some metals, metal oxides, metal sulfide, Metal organic frameworks (MOFs), and carbon-based nanomaterials can mimic the properties of natural peroxidase and convert H2O2 into highly toxic·OH. The generated hydroxyl radicals play many key roles in biological systems, such as sensing, treating cancer and defending against pathogens [33–35]. Some nanozyme-based composite materials just rely on the peroxidase activity of nanozymes to achieve anti-bacteria and anti-biofilm functions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Based on the oxidase-like activity of nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: Oxidases are the main enzymes in peroxisomes, and oxidases account for about half of the total peroxisomes. Various oxidases act on different substrates, however, their common feature is that at the same time as oxidizing the substrate, oxygen is reduced to water or hydrogen peroxide (Scheme 2 ) [32]. So, oxidase must play a catalytic role in the presence of oxygen. Some nanoparticles included metals, metal oxides, MOFs, and carbon-based materials can mimic the properties of natural oxidase and can reduce oxygen into superoxide anions while oxidizing substrates. The produced superoxide anion causes damage to cells and also bacteria and biofilms, which is typically used for cancer treatment and defense of pathogens [32]. Therefore, nanozymes with oxidase activity can be used as a functional part of composite materials for anti-bacteria and anti-biofilm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Based on the haloperoxidase-like activity of nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: Haloperoxidase belongs to peroxidase in nature, however, it is different from conventional peroxidase. According to the different types of halogens, haloperoxidase can be divided into three types: chloroperoxidase, bromoperoxidase, and iodoperoxidase [36]. Haloperoxidase can oxidize halide ions to hypohalous acid in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (Scheme 3 ) [37]. Hypohalous acid has strong oxidizing ability, which can damage bacteria and can play a role of biological antifouling effect [38]. Some nanozymes are able to mimic the activity of haloperoxidase and become components of composite materials for anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Typical types of nanozymes for fighting against bacteria and biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes have functions of anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms. The essence is that these types of nanomaterials mimic the catalytic activity of natural enzymes which can catalyze substrate reactions to produce harmful ROS causing irreversible damage to bacteria. Namely, they possess any one or more enzyme activities of peroxidase, oxidase and haloperoxidase. Typical ones include metals, metal oxides, metal sulfide, carbon-based nanomaterials, and MOFs family (Fig. 2 ) [39].

Metal nanomaterials are potential oxidase and peroxidase mimic, especially precious metals, transition metals, and bimetals [40–47]. At present, researches on anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms of metal nanozymes mainly include gold nanomaterials, palladium nanomaterials, platinum nanomaterials, etc.

Tao Y et al. loaded gold nanoparticles onto bifunctional mesoporous silica by in situ reduction of HAuCl4 with NaBH4 (MSN-AuNPs), and used the inherent oxidase and peroxidase catalytic activities for anti-bacteria [48]. The research found that MSN-AuNPs had excellent oxidase and peroxidase catalytic activities in a wide range of pH, temperature and ionic strength, which was more conducive to the development of antibacterial effects under physiological conditions. The peroxidase activity of MSN-AuNPs converted hydrogen peroxide to hydroxyl radicals (
OH), and the oxidase activity converted oxygen and other substrates to singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide anions (O2
−), which had good antibacterial effect both on Gram-positive bacteria and Gram-negative bacteria. In addition, it was found that MSN-AuNPs could inhibit the formation of biofilms and could eliminate the biofilms that already formed.

Fang G et al. found that palladium nanocrystals synthesized by a hydrothermal method had oxidase and peroxidase-like activities, and both of them were higher in Pd cubes than in Pd octahedra (Fig. 3 a, b) [49]. Pd nanocrystals could effectively catalyze the oxidation of biological related antioxidants to generate hydrogen peroxide which was then converted into highly active hydroxyl radicals to effectively kill bacteria. In addition, for killing Gram-positive bacteria, they found that the anti-bacterial efficiency of Pd nanocrystals was positively related to lattice plane. Specifically, the {100} faceted Pd cubes with higher enzyme activity were more effective than the {111} faceted Pd octahedra for fighting against bacteria (Fig. 3d and f). However, for killing Gram-negative bacteria, the anti-bacteria performance and enzyme activity showed a reverse trend (Fig. 3c and e). Although Pd cubes had higher enzyme activity than Pd octahedra, Pd octahedra had the stronger penetrating power in bacterial membrane than Pd cubes, which led to Pd octahedra possessed the higher antibacterial activity than Pd cubes. Hence, it proved that both the crystal facet and geometry determined the anti-bacteria activity of Pd nanocrystals.

Cai S et al. used a facile and low-cost dealloying process to prepared to the porous dealloyed Pt50/Ag50 nanoparticles from conventional octahedra PtxAg100-x (x = 25, 50, 75) [50]. They also investigated the multifunctional enzyme activity and antibacterial properties of mesoporous Pt/Ag nanoparticles. As a result, the material of Pt50/Ag50 showed the best oxidase, peroxidase and catalase activities compared with the same series of materials. The oxidase and peroxidase activities of Pt50/Ag50 resulted in this material had good antibacterial effects on both E. coli and S. aureus.
Many metal oxide-based nanomaterials had also been explored to mimic functional enzymes in natural defense systems.

Natalio F et al. studied the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of V2O5 nanowires synthesized by a hydrothermal method, and showed that V2O5 nanowires acted like naturally occurring vanadium haloxidase which could prevent marine biological pollution [51]. They found that in the presence of bromide and hydrogen peroxide, V2O5 nanowires could catalyze the oxidation of bromide to hypobromous acid (HOBr). During the process, singlet oxygen (1O2) could also be produced. HOBr and 1O2 had strong biocidal effects on both S. aureus and E. coli.

Herget K et al. developed CeO2x nanorods synthesized by a hydrothermal method as haloperoxidase mimics that based on Ce4+/Ce3+ redox couples [52]. In the presence of H2O2, bromine ions could be oxidized by CeO2x nanorods to form HOBr which reacted with signaling molecules involved in bacterial quorum sensing (ie N-(3-oxoacyl) homoserine lactone) to form halogenated products, thereby achieving resistance to biological pollution.

On the basis of CuO-Fenton reaction (peroxidase), Wang L et al. systematically studied the effect of chloride ions on the inhibition and removal of biofilms by CuO peroxidase [53]. After the addition of chloride ions, the complexation with copper accelerated the CuO-Fenton reaction. Chloride ions as a reducing agent (or hydroxy scavenger) were oxidized to form active chlorine which was an effective component for removing biological membranes. Additionally, herring sperm DNA, bovine serum protein, and chitosan was used to simulate extracellular polymers, which proved that the system could degraded the components of biofilm extracellular polymers such as polysaccharides, proteins, and nucleic acids. At the same time, the biofilm formed by Pseudomonas aeruginosa was used as the research object, and it proved that the Cl-CuO-H2O2 system could clear the biofilm, kill the bacteria in the biofilm, and prevent the biofilm rebuilding.

Gao L et al. synthesized Fe3O4 (MNPs) via one-step in a solvothermal system, and had studied the role of MNPs with peroxidase activity to cleavage biological macromolecules for eliminate biofilms [54]. It was found that MNPs enhanced the oxidative cleavage of biofilm components in the presence of H2O2. Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PA01) was used as model strain to prove that the MNP-H2O2 system could effectively destroy biofilms already formed, prevent the formation of new biofilms, kill planktonic bacteria, and kill bacteria in biofilms.

Bukhari S et al. found that iron oxide nanoparticles (IO-NPs) had peroxidase activity and might be applied in endodontic disinfection to improve the antibacterial activity of root canals surface and dentina tubules [55]. They used the root canal surfaces and dentinal tubules from single-rooted intact extracted teeth infected by Enterococcus faecalis for 3 weeks as vitro model, which successfully recapitulated biofilm formation and dentinal tubules infection. It proved that the H2O2-IO-NPs system could kill more Enterococcus faecalis on the surface of the root canal and on the dentinal tubules of different depths.

Karim MN et al. found that CuO nanorods synthesized by a solution-based approach had peroxidase activity, and controlled the antibacterial activity of the CuO nanorods nanozyme by using visible light as an external "trigger" [56]. Illumination could enhance the affinity of CuO nanorods for H2O2 so as to adjust the catalytic performance of peroxide mimic enzymes, and made the production of ROS significantly accelerated 20 times. The physical damage caused by ROS led to rapid degeneration of bacteria.
Among metal sulfide, MoS2 nanoparticles and FeS nanoparticles had been widely used in anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms research as they had peroxidase activity and relatively low cytotoxicity.

Yang X et al. studied the antibacterial properties of two-dimensional (2D) chemically exfoliated MoS2 (ce-MoS2) nanosheets [57]. It has found that ce-MoS2 nanosheets could generate ROS. The damage induced by the superoxide anion (O2
–) that produced by ce-MoS2 and the subsequent oxidation which not depends on the superoxide anion together played antibacterial effects. Due to the two-dimensional planar structure (high specific surface area) and high electrical conductivity, the antibacterial activity of ce-MoS2 nanosheets was much stronger than the original MoS2 powder which used to synthesize ce-MoS2 nanosheets.

In addition, there were many researches on anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms removal of nano-FeS. Xu Z et al. had prepared FeS derived from natural organic sulfur compounds by solvothermal method, which had good effects for combating pathogenic bacteria, killing drug-resistant bacteria, and removing biofilms [58]. FeS killed bacteria by releasing hydrogen polysulfanes, and its peroxidase activity could accelerate the release of hydrogen polysulfanes.

Carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon quantum dots (GQDs), and graphene oxides (GO) could also mimic the activities of natural peroxidase [59–62], all of which were promised for combating bacteria and biofilms.

Wang H et al. used one-pot oxidation reflux method synthesized oxidized carbon nanotubes (o-CNTs) to simulate natural peroxidase [63]. Through the combination of theoretical calculations and experiments, it proved that the enzymatic activity of o-CNTs comes from the rich oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface. The group was further reduced the number of carboxyl groups on the surface through chemical methods thereby increasing the catalytic activity of o-CNTs. Because o-CNTs could mimic peroxidase under physiological conditions and catalyzed the production of hydroxyl radicals by H2O2, the group combined o-CNTs and a trace amount of H2O2 for the treatment of wound bacterial infections.

Sun H et al. proposed an antibacterial system with low levels of H2O2 and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) which synthesized from graphite by a modified Hummers method [64]. They found the peroxidase-like activity of GQDs could convert H2O2 into highly toxic ·OH, which could improve the antibacterial performance of H2O2. The GQDs-H2O2 system had antibacterial activity for fighting against both Gram-negative (E. coli) and Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) bacteria, and could exhibit excellent antibacterial performance with the help of low-dose H2O2.

Song Y et al. reported that carboxyl modified graphene oxide (GO-COOH) has inherent peroxidase activity [65]. Although it is applied to glucose detection, the GO-COOH has the potential to be used for anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms according to the design principle of nanozyme-based composite materials for combating bacteria/biofilms. Moreover, such as hemin-graphene [66] and Au nanocluster (NC)–graphene oxide [67] also has inherent peroxidase activity and has the potential to be used for anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms.

Metal-organic framework (MOFs) were an interesting class of porous crystalline materials, which were constructed by coordination of metal ions/cluster with organic linkers [68,69]. The regular arrangement of metal nodes and organic ligands in MOFs provided abundant accessible catalytic sites and gave MOFs intrinsic enzyme mimic properties. In recent years, MOFs nanozymes have been widely used in chemical sensing and biosensing [70–72]. For example, Xu W et al. prepared Fe-MIL-88B-NH2 with peroxidase-like activity, ultra-high stability and high biocompatibility by solvothermal method. Then Fe-MIL-88B-NH2 was used as the carrier of glucose oxidase (GOx) to establish an integrated enzyme system by amination coupling reaction [73], and cascade catalytic glucose colorimetric detection was performed. Due to the "nano-scale proximity effect", H2O2 generated by GOx-catalyzed glucose oxidation with O2 would be immediately oxidized in situ by Fe-MIL-88B-NH2. The system (Fe-MOF-GOx) for glucose detection not only showed strong temperature adaptability and resistance to acid and alkali, but also had unexpectedly high reusability. Hu Z et al. prepared UiO-66 (NH2) with an effective peroxidase mimicking activity by a fast microwave-assisted method, which could be used for colorimetric detection of biothiolin including Cys, GSH and Hcy under mild conditions and without H2O2 [74]. It was worth noting that metal oxide clusters within UiO-66 (NH2) nanostructures can exhibit semiconductor characteristics, and organic ligands can serve as antennas for collecting photons. Under light-emitting diode (LED) irradiation, UiO-66 (NH2) showed good photocatalytic performance. Imitating natural peroxidase, Xu B et al. designed and synthesized a highly active single-atom nanozyme containing Zn porphyrin structure, which used ZIF-8 as a precursor and mesoporous silicon protection strategy [75]. A series of experiments had confirmed that the monoatomic Zn in the Zn porphyrin structure was the key to their enzyme-like activity. Through theoretical calculations, it was revealed that their active structures are an unsaturated Zn-N4 structure of a Zn porphyrin. And the single-atom nanozyme could catalyze H2O2 to produce ·OH to kill bacteria. In vitro anti-bacteria experiment, the single-atom nanozyme achieved a bacteriostatic rate of up to 99.85 %, and at the same time, it could effectively promote wound healing for mouse wound infection model at low concentrations of H2O2 (Fig. 4 ).

In short, Fe-based MOF, Zr-based MOF, and other metal-based MOF nanozymes have endogenous peroxidase activity [76–78]. Although there were hardly any studies on anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms, they are still potential nanozymes for combating bacteria and biofilms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research progress of nanozyme-based composite materials in combating bacteria and biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections are an increasingly serious global problem, and the main response is using antibiotics. However, the abuse and misuse of antibiotics have led to more and more drug resistance of bacteria, which can affect the therapeutic effect and lead to higher mortality rates [79]. This situation prompts the development of antibacterial materials [80–84].

The properties of oxidase or peroxidase that can transform O2 or H2O2 into ROS endow nanozyme with anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms activity. Although nanozymes are excellent potential alternatives for antibiotics, using nanozymes to combat bacteria and biofilms have many inherent drawbacks. For example, almost all nanozymes cannot effectively interact with bacteria, moreover ROS has the disadvantages of intrinsic short lifetime and short diffusion distance [23,24], which will reduce the sterilization effect; anti-bacteria of nanozyme has no selectivity, and their lack controlled switches.

The components of composite materials are usually very different in structure, physical properties, and chemical properties. Their combination and synergy make them more diverse in function and biological activity. If nanozymes compounded with other elements to form composite materials, the above-mentioned defects may be overcome while the advantages of nanozyme are brought into play. At the same time, multiple functions can be integrated into a single system to realize an intelligent multifunctional antibacterial treatment mode.

Biofilm refers to the organized and structured bacterial aggregates adhered to the surface by bacteria self-secreted extracellular polymers including polysaccharides, lipids, proteins, and extracellular DNA (eDNA) [85]. Extracellular polymers of bacteria biofilm can form a physical shield, which delays the penetration of antibacterial drugs through surface passivation; meanwhile, hydrolases and eDNA in extracellular polymers can make antibiotics rendered ineffective by hydrolyzing antibiotics or chelating cationic antibiotics; in addition, extracellular polymers protect bacteria from attacks by the host's immune system [86]. Therefore, the tolerance of biofilm to bactericide and host's immune system is approximately 10–1000 times to that of corresponding free bacteria. The removal of biofilms is still a challenge for many industries. Fortunately, some nanozyme-based composites can inhibit the formation of biofilms and can eliminate biofilms that have formed.

Under the background of the emergence and rapid spread of drug-resistant bacteria and the slow development of new antibiotic products, nanozyme-based composite materials have great application prospects in the field of anti-bacteria/biofilms, and a lot of researches has been accomplished (see Table 1 ).

Yin W et al. developed an antibacterial composite material based on polyethylene glycol functionalized molybdenum disulfide nanoflowers (PEG-MoS2 NFs) [87]. On the one hand, the peroxidase activity of PEG-MoS2 NFs could effectively catalyze the decomposition of low-concentration H2O2 to generate
OH which had high anti-bacteria activity. And the production of
OH made the resistant bacteria more fragile making the wound easier to heal. On the other hand, MoS2 in the PEG-MoS2 NFs composite material had good photothermal effects (NIR) and could assist anti-bacteria. PEG-MoS2 NFs combined enzyme catalysis with NIR to provide a new strategy for the rapid and effective killing ampicillin resistant E. coli and endospore-forming Bacillus subtilis in vitro. Interestingly, 808 nm irradiation induced hyperthermia could accelerate the oxidation of glutathione (GSH) in the presence of PEG-MoS2 NFs. Thus, the synergistic antibacterial system could effectively kill bacteria, promoted the scar generation to protect the wound tissue, and further modulated the collagen alignment.

Niu J et al. designed an intelligent Gram-selective antibacterial system based on molybdenum disulfide and photoacid molecules (Cit-MoS2) [88]. Through light irradiation, this system could modulate surface charge conversion of MoS2 and changed the pH which could activate the enzyme activity of MoS2. Considering the differences in cell wall composition and structure of different strains, Gram-selective anti-bacteria could be achieved by controlling the light irradiation time for this system (Fig. 5 ). This work provided new ideas for the realization of selective anti-bacteria through simple and effective light regulation. Additionally, in vivo experiments showed negligible side effects of this nanozyme system on major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidney).

Subsequently, Sang Y et al. designed a composite dressing based on molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and hydrogel, which could effectively capture and eliminate bacteria [89]. This composite dressing used enzymatic activity and photothermal effects of MoS2 to achieve antibacterial functions, while using hydrogel to capture bacteria. This composite dressing enhanced the interaction between nanozymes and bacteria, and reduced the risk of wound infection when eliminating bacteria. Moreover, H&E staining of the main organs from the mice treating the wound with the material after 5 days was found that there were no obvious accumulated of material and inflammation in all organs.

Liu X et al. choose ultra-thin two-dimensional MOF nanosheets as peroxidase mimics then physically adsorbed glucose oxidase (GOx) to obtain hybrid nanocatalysts as benign, self-activating cascade reagents [90], in which non-toxic glucose was converted into rich gluconic acid and H2O2 avoiding the direct use of relatively high concentrations and toxic H2O2 and minimizing harmful side effects. The production of gluconic acid could lower the pH value from 7 to 3–4, thereby significantly activating the peroxidase-like activity of the two-dimensional MOF nanosheets. Moreover, results showed that the designed benign, self-activating cascade reagents had powerful anti-bacteria effects and negligible biological toxicity. The group also studied the biodistribution of the hybrid nanocatalyst. The results demonstrated that most 2D MOF/GOx nanosheets accumulated in the liver and spleen and only a small amount of 2D MOF/GOx accumulated in the heart, kidney, and lung. Meanwhile, it was found that the hybrid catalyst could be cleared from the body as time progressed.

Ji H et al. loaded ascorbic acid (AA) into the channels of graphene and mesoporous silicon (GS), after that encapsulated it with hyaluronic acid-dopamine (HA-DA) linker. Then the high affinity of Fe3O4 with dopamine was utilized to connected Fe3O4 to the surface of GS to formed a smart on-demand nanozyme/photothermal synergistic antibacterial system (Fig. 6 ) [91]. Once it reached the infection site, HA-DA was degraded by the hyaluronidase which was secreted by the bacteria. The AA in the GS channels were released and then catalyzed by Fe3O4 to generated highly toxic
OH. In addition, the graphene in this antibacterial system has photothermal effect which combines with
OH had a strong antibacterial effect. Moreover, no obvious histological lesion or adverse effect was found under their experimental conditions, which indicated the on-demand drug delivery in the bacterial infection site.

Owing to CeO2 nanozyme showed SOD and catalase activity under neutral and alkaline conditions while peroxidase activity under acidic conditions, Xu C et al. assembled CeO2 nanozyme, photothermal agent (graphene oxide), and photobase reagent (malachite green methanol base) into a hydrogel constructed an intelligent optical switch system [92]. Before ultraviolet light irradiation, the peroxidase activity of CeO2 nanozyme was activated by the initial acidic environment of the system and the antibacterial effect was generated; after ultraviolet light irradiation, the system became neutral or alkali which activated the SOD and catalase activities of CeO2 nanozyme to produce antioxidant effects. Therefore, oxidation or anti-oxidation performance of materials could be controlled through ultraviolet light, which could achieve anti-bacteria in the first stage and anti-inflammatory in the second stage.

Liu J et al. loaded selenium nanoparticles (Se NPs) functionalized with Ru complexes into gelatin nanoparticles (GNPs) and coated them with red blood cell membranes (RBCM) to construct a biomimetic multifunctional anti-bacteria nanosystem (Ru-Se@GNP-RBCM) [93]. After the system accumulated in the bacterial infection area, the exotoxin secreted by the bacteria was absorbed, and the GNPs were degraded by gelatinase. Then, Ru-Se NPs was released to achieve synergistic anti-bacteria and real-time in vivo imaging. Besides, in the early stage, the group connected the antibacterial peptide UBI29-41 (PEP) with functionalized Se NPs and then coated Ru complexes on to their outer layers to construct antibacterial system [94]. Although there is no mention of the activity of mimic enzymes, the antibacterial mechanism of Se system against bacteria was that Se particles entered the interior of bacterial cells and produced ROS in situ.

Yan Z et al. prepared H2O2-free antibacterial system by loading CaO2 and heme-loaded graphene (H-G) into alginate (CaO2/H-G@alginate) [95]. When the composite material was implanted near the site of bacterial infection, water infiltrated to the material from the site of bacterial infection first reacted with CaO2 to generated H2O2. And then heme as a peroxidase mimic catalyzed the intermediate H2O2 generated
OH to treat bacterial infections and destroy the main components of the biofilm. The hybridization of heme and graphene in this material could improve stability and catalytic activity. In addition, Ca2+ cross-linked polymerized sodium alginate matrix had been widely used as a biocompatible hydrogel. The closed environment of the system could reduce the potential risk of extra H2O2 addition and increase the ability of anti-biofilms.

In addition, Yan Z et al. implanted catheters (an implant-related periprosthetic infection model, PPI) and samples in subcutaneous of the mice [95]. Seven days later, the wounds of the CaO2/H-G@alginate group formed scabs and gradually healed. However, significant swelling and purulence were observed in the infected site of the control group. Then, the catheter was taken out and they found that the catheter of the control group was contaminated, which proved that the biofilm still remained on the surface. But the catheters in the CaO2/H-G@alginate group hardly decolorized, which indicated that there was less biofilm remaining on them. Observed by SEM, a large amount of biofilm covered the surface of the embedded catheter in the control group, on the contrary, no obvious biofilm or bacteria were observed after the sample CaO2/H-G@alginate treatment. The above results indicated that CaO2/H-G@alginate samples could effectively treat biofilms on medical devices.

Wang Z et al. combined AuNPs with ultra-thin graphite carbonitride (g-C3N4) to prepare anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms system [96]. This system could kill bacteria, disinfect wounds, and treat lung infections at biologically relevant concentrations of H2O2. The synergistic effect of AuNPs and g-C3N4 made the peroxidase activity of material significantly improved, which could more effectively catalyze H2O2 generated
OH. In vitro experiments proved that the system not only showed amazing bactericidal performance for combating multidrug resistant Gram-negative bacteria and Gram-positive bacteria, but also showed high efficiency in decomposing multidrug resistant biofilms that had existed and preventing the formation of new biofilms. This work was an important step in obtaining novel nanozymes with multiple functions and reactivity.

Chen Z et al. fabricated DNase-mimetic artificial enzyme (DMAE) by physically adsorbing AuNPs on the surface of colloidal magnetic Fe3O4/SiO2 core/shell particles and then assembling a single layer of Ce(IV) nitrilotriacetic acid (NTA) composite on the bare AuNP [97], which had high cleavage capacity for model substrates and natural DNA, and could inhibit biofilm formation in 120 h (Fig. 7 ). Destroying the integrity of extracellular polymeric substances through DMAE greatly improved the ability of antibiotics to kill bacteria hidden in the biofilm, thereby eliminating the biofilm. Besides, the presence of magnetic Fe3O4 nuclei made the DMAE recyclable. Compared with the high sensitivity to the environment of natural DNase, DMAE had better operational stability and easier obtained. The application of DMAE would provide unprecedented opportunities for anti-biofilms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages of the nanozyme-based composite materials for combating bacteria and biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with traditional antibiotics or biological antibacterial agents, nanozymes as new types of antibacterial agents mimic natural enzymes presenting good application potential in combating bacteria and biofilms with many advantages. Such as, nanozymes are mainly synthesized by chemical methods, which are simple to prepare, low cost, high stability, and various functions; by controlling factors such as size and morphology, the catalytic ability of nanozymes can be adjusted and optimized; the specificity of nanozyme can be conferred through the combination of nanozymes and specific recognition molecules; the anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms conditions of nanozyme are milder and less prone to drug resistance. Further, the composite materials formed by nanozymes and other components can play a synergistic role in better utilize the advantages of nanozymes.

Natural enzymes have high efficiency catalytic capabilities. Whether nanozymes can replace natural enzymes well depends on their catalytic ability. The catalytic ability of nanozymes can be adjusted. The size, shape and morphology, composition, surface coating and modification all affects the catalytic activity of nanozymes. For example, Peng F et al. studied the size dependence of Fe3O4MNPs on the peroxidase activity they exhibit [98]. By comparing the peroxidase activity of Fe3O4MNPs with different diameters, they found that the peroxidase activity increased as the size of the nanoparticles decreased. Vernekar AA et al. prepared MnFe2O4 nanoparticles with different morphologies including nano octahedra bound by {111} planes, nano-sheets and nano-wires by changing the synthesis conditions [99]. They studied the morphology effect of MnFe2O4 on its oxidase activity and found that nano octahedra bound by {111} planes had stronger oxidase activity than nanosheets and nanowires. He W et al. prepared Ag-related bimetal alloys including AgAu, AgPd and AgPt [100]. By comparing the peroxidase activity of the three bimetal alloys, they found the peroxidase activity could be adjusted by alloy composition. Fan K et al. synthesized histidine-modified Fe3O4 (His-Fe3O4) nanoparticles by solvothermal method [101]. The addition of histidine increased the affinity for H2O2 through formation of hydrogen bonds between histidine imidazolyl and H2O2, thereby improved the peroxidase activity and catalytic efficiency of Fe3O4 nanozymes.

In addition, the catalytic activity of nanozymes can be adjusted by changing the environment such as pH and temperature, adding activators and inhibitors, and lighting [102–105]. The amount of produced ROS can be indirectly controlled by adjusting the activity of nanozymes, which is beneficial to combating bacteria/biofilms.

Nanozyme itself has poor selectivity, however it is nanomaterials which can assemble small molecules such as antibodies, peptides, aptamers, and nucleic acid probes that can specifically recognize pathogens to achieve targeted cell internal transport or targeted sterilization. Drugs or chemical molecules can also be assembled on nanozyme to achieve selectivity. For instance, in view of the differences of cell wall composition and structure of different strains, the combination of molybdenum disulfide and photoacid molecules could build an intelligent Gram-selective antibacterial system. This system could regulate the surface charge conversion of molybdenum disulfide through the irradiation time of ultraviolet light and adjust the pH of the system to activate the enzyme activity of molybdenum disulfide to achieve Gram-selective anti-bacteria. When the ultraviolet light irradiation time was short, the material had a strong exterminate action on Gram-positive bacteria. And after prolonging the ultraviolet light irradiation time, the material had a strong exterminate action on Gram-negative bacteria [88].

In addition to the enzyme catalytic activity, nanozymes may also have photothermal, magnetic, and fluorescent characteristics. The multiple functions of nanozyme can work together to make composite material more effective in removing bacteria and biofilms. At the same time, magnetic and fluorescence characteristics make the nanozyme-based composite materials easy to separate, and may be used for imaging contrast agents to facilitate monitoring. For example, Fe3O4 nanozyme has both peroxidase activity and magnetism. The enzyme activity plays a catalytic role, meanwhile magnetism can be used as an imaging contrast agent and can be easily separated after used. AuNPs nanozyme has the peroxidase activity and surface-enhanced Raman scattering characteristics. The use of appropriate Pt shells can enhance the nanozyme activity while retaining the Raman characteristics of AuNPs, resulting in sensitivity increased about 1–2 orders of magnitude and detection time shortened [106]. MoS2 nanozyme has peroxidase activity and photothermal effect. Combining the peroxidase activity and photothermal effect can greatly improve the anti-bacteria performance of the material [89].

There are roughly three ways to produce bacterial resistance: producing inactivated enzymes that can catalyze the binding of certain groups to the OH or NH2 groups of antibiotics leading to inactivate of antibiotics; changing the permeability of bacterial cell membranes to make it difficult for antibacterial drugs to enter the bacteria; or the structure of the target site in the bacteria is changed so that the drug cannot be easily combine with it [107]. Through their own redox enzyme activity, nanozymes generate high-toxic ROS in the process of catalyzing non-toxic or low-toxic substrate reactions. It is difficult for bacteria to fully adapt to this treatment, so nanozyme-based composite materials for anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms are not easy to develop resistance, which will be very advantageous.

The bactericidal efficiency of nanozyme-based materials is obvious. Zhang D et al. found that a trace amount of iron oxide nanozyme could kill 100 % of E. coli under the optimal concentration of H2O2, however the sterilization efficiency of H2O2 alone was less than 15 % [108].Wang Z et al. found that graphitic carbon nitride loaded with gold nanoparticles (g-C3N4@Au NPs, CNA) could be injected into mice to treat lung-resistant bacteria infections [96]. The bactericidal efficiency was comparable to vancomycin. In addition, Sun H et al. found that the survival rate of E. coli and S. aureus was reduced to less than 10 % in the presence of graphene quantum dot nanozyme and H2O2 [67]. Cai S et al. found that platinum-silver alloy octahedra (Pt/Ag nanoparticles) nanozyme could kill more than 90 % of E. coli and S. aureus [50].

Compared with photodynamic antibacterial system, the nanozyme-based composite materials rely on their inherent redox enzyme activity to catalyze the generation of a large number of ROS without light irradiation to achieving anti-bacteria.

Additionally, nanozymes are mostly inorganic nanoparticles synthesized by simple chemical methods. Therefore, the production cost of nanozymes is much lower while the stability is much higher than natural enzymes, which suit for mass production and have strong resistance to harsh environments. Through the design of the synthetic process, it is easy to obtain nanozymes with different sizes, different morphologies, and specific group modification, which is conducive to the composite with other components to prepare intelligent multi-mode anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges of the nanozyme-based composite materials for combating bacteria and biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: Both cytotoxicity and animal safety are one of important indicators for screening and evaluating material safety. Nanozymes are potential ideal substitutes for antibiotics, of course the assessment of cytotoxicity and animal safety have received widespread attention. The potential cytotoxicity and animal safety of nanozyme-based composites for anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms is derived from nanozyme and composite components.

Nanozymes are nanomaterials entering the organism mainly through the respiratory system, skin contact, food and injection, and then interact with the cells directly. The cytotoxicity of nanozymes must be strictly evaluated. Several reagents based on iron oxide nanomaterials have been approved for clinical use. However, studies have shown that some nanomaterials have certain cytotoxicity after entering the organism. The cytotoxicity of nanomaterials is closely related to their own physical and chemical properties and the types of cells that are acting. The cytotoxicity of nanomaterials also shows a significant concentration dependence. For example, graphene oxide (GO) nanomaterials have good biocompatibility but also some cytotoxicity. Hu W et al. used A549 cells which cultured in a medium containing different concentrations of fetal bovine serum as a model to studied the cytotoxicity of GO nanosheets [109]. They found that the cytotoxicity of GO was obviously concentration dependent. And it proved that the physical damage of GO nanosheets to the cell membrane was the main factor for its cytotoxicity. Xu M et al. studied cytotoxicity effect of surface modified GO on cells and found that GO after surface modification had significantly different cytotoxicity [110]. Unmodified GO could destroy the integrity and affected the function of the cell membrane, while modified GO with polyacrylic acid had very little cytotoxicity. Some nanozymes may also have certain toxic on animals. At present, more toxicity researches are focused on mammalian models such as rats and mice. The toxicity of nanozymes to animals is closely related to its action position, action mode and action concentration, as well as its own size and surface functional groups. In addition, based on their own redox enzyme activity, nanozymes catalyze non-toxic or low-toxic substrate reactions to generate highly toxic ROS to sterilize or destroy biofilms. Highly toxic ROS will inevitably cause certain damage to the cells when sterilizing and destroying the biofilm.
The cytotoxicity and animal safety of the components that together forms the composite material with the nanozyme will also make the overall material toxic and thus limit their use.

Besides possible cytotoxicity and animal safety, the low catalytic activity, lack of targeting, and poor selectivity of nanozymes compared with natural enzymes led to poor anti-bacteria and anti-biofilm performance of the nanozyme-based composite materials are also challenges. Specificity is the fundamental characteristic of natural enzymes, which mainly determines the high efficiency of catalysis. The weak substrate specificity of nanozymes provides poor selectivity on anti-bacteria/anti-biofilms. On the other hand, certain nanozymes have multiple enzymatic activities. However, in some cases, an inevitable catalytic property will cause some potential side effects, which should be carefully studied to obtain an effective therapeutic window.

In order to promote the clinical application of nanozymes, both their benefits and risks should be systematically evaluated. Such research includes, but is not limited to, the evaluation of nanozymes' cell fate, clinical toxicity, pharmacokinetics, and immunogenicity. The long-term impact on the environment should also be considered. There are many types of nanozymes and nanozyme-based composite materials for combating bacteria/biofilms, so the treatment methods and environmental impact after use should be considered according to their types.

In this review, we have summarized the general mechanisms and the design principles of nanozyme-based composite materials for combating bacteria and biofilms, the typical types of nanozymes for fighting against bacteria and biofilms, and the research progress of nanozyme-based composite materials in the field of anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms. Then their advantages and challenges have been analyzed. Due to the simple of preparation, high stability, and various functions, the application researches of nanozyme-based composite materials in the field of anti-bacteria and anti-biofilms have been increasing in recent years, and significant achievements have been made in the development of enzyme mimics against bacteria/biofilms.

However, the field of nanozyme-based composite materials for combating bacteria/biofilms is still in its infancy, and many problems and challenges still need to be solved. 1) Compared with the diversity of natural enzymes, the design principle (simulating redox enzyme activity) of composite materials based on nanozyme for combating bacteria/biofilms is relatively poor, so other catalytic activities should be explored in the future; 2) Compared with the specificity of natural enzymes, most nanozyme-based composite materials have poor selectivity. When designing anti-bacteria and anti-biofilm systems based on nanozymes, it is suggested that should pay attention to adding targeting and specific elements to improve selectivity; 3) Most nanozymes need to be in an acidic environment to activate redox enzyme activity, which is not conducive to the biological application of nanozyme-based composite materials. It is necessary to study whether the nanozyme can be activated under physiological conditions by surface modification; 4) The nature of nanozymes is nanomaterials, so the biotoxicity of nanozymes needs further evaluation. Compounding the nanozyme with other biocompatible components may reduce the biotoxicity of the nanozyme itself. Future breakthroughs in nanozyme technology will lead to a new era in the field of against bacteria and biofilms.
